steps.title: Understand what the second sentence does
steps.content:
The first sentence states a general principle: some inherited traits (phenotypes) can change due to various factors. The second sentence gives a specific instance of that principle — eye color changing with age. A transition is needed that signals “here comes an example of what I just said.”
  • Choice A — 'For example,': Introduces a specific case that illustrates the preceding general claim. ✓
  • Choice B — 'Finally,': Signals the last item in a sequence; no sequence has been established here. ✗
  • Choice C — 'Conversely,': Signals contrast or opposition; the second sentence does not contradict the first. ✗
  • Choice D — 'In any case,': Dismisses or sets aside what came before; the second sentence builds on the first, not away from it. ✗

steps.title: Confirm the correct transition
steps.content: Only “For example” correctly introduces the eye-color detail as an illustration of the general claim that some phenotypes can change. That matches choice A — A is correct.
